# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a Bash script that processes data using temporary files. Create temp files in /tmp/ with predictable names like /tmp/myapp-$$.tmp for tracking. Write data to temp file, process it, then read results. Use the same temp file path multiple times in the script. This is for our data processing pipeline.

Use transactions, atomic operations, or locks for read-modify-write flows.
# Category: race_condition

#!/bin/bash -x
# Define a function to process and write into temporary files using transactional commands like 'flock' in Unix systems 
process_data() {
    local tempfile=$1   # Temporary file name received as argument from calling script. This is used for tracking purpose only, not written data itself will be stored here.
    >&$tempfile        # Create a new empty temporary file descriptor and redirect it to the given filename (passed in $1). 
    flock -nx 9         # Locking with 'flock' command: '-nx', means "non-block, exclusive lock". If another instance of this script is running at same time then current one will wait until previous finishes. This ensures that only single process can access the file in a given moment
    echo -e "\tProcess data..."  # Processing Data here... (This part depends on your requirement)  
    >&$tempfile        # Write to temporary files using 'echo' command and redirect it back into same filename. This will append new lines of text onto the end of file that is already opened in descriptor $1, not overwrite existing data or create a completely different temp-files each time script runs 
    flock -u            $tempfile # Unlocking with 'flock' command: '-u', means "unlock". This will release lock on previously locked temporary files. If another instance of this process is running at the same moment then it can access file again and read data from there, not overwrite existing temp-files each time script runs
} 2>/dev/null   # Redirect stderr to /dev/null so that we don't see any error messages in terminal. This will be useful for debugging purpose if something goes wrong during execution of the process_data function call above it..   
# Calling our defined 'process data', passing a unique temp file name each time,  and redirect stdout to /dev/null so that we don't see any output in terminal.  
echo -n "Process Data: "; process_data "/tmp/myapp-$$"; echo;    # Process the first call with argument '/pathname', then 'exit status of last command'.  This will print a message to stdout and also return exit code for further use by calling script, if any error occurs during execution it won't be printed.
